title.rendered: City throws support behind Paint Branch Chinese immersion program

excerpt.rendered: The College Park City Council voted Tuesday to send a letter to the Prince George’s County Board of Education (BOE) in support of continued funding for the Chinese immersion program at Paint Branch Elementary School. The letter came two days before the board is scheduled to vote on its proposed fiscal 2027 budget and follows […]

organizations: Prince George’s County Board of Education
extraction.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted to send a letter to the Prince George’s County Board of Education in support of continued funding for the Chinese immersion program at Paint Branch Elementary School. The program has become an important part of the broader educational ecosystem serving College Park residents, and changes that significantly reduce immersion opportunities could undermine years of investment by educators, students and the community. Paint Branch PTA Secretary Alexandra Tyukavina proposed a third alternative to address budget issues, adding an opt-out option and devoting one full-day English-speaking teacher to students not wishing to take lessons in Mandarin, stating 'We, the Paint Branch school community, have found that the board’s proposal to eliminate the program is not the only way to address the budget issues, nor is it ideal for the students and families within the school'.

title.rendered: Council adopts early lease ordinance

excerpt.rendered: The College Park City Council on Feb. 3 unanimously approved an ordinance that will prevent landlords from asking tenants to renew a lease sooner than 180 days from the end of the current lease. The so-called early lease ordinance, overwhelmingly supported by students renting apartments in high-rise buildings on or near Route 1, was introduced […]

extraction.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously approved an ordinance that prevents landlords from asking tenants to renew a lease sooner than 180 days from the end of the current lease. Sami Saeed, a University of Maryland public policy major, shared his story of being approached by his former landlord just four weeks after moving in with a substantial rent increase, saying 'The stress that that caused me … trying to find anyone to live with for next year. I had to call 50 people.' The ordinance, which was amended to count the timeframe from the end of the lease instead of the beginning, aims to protect tenants from aggressive lease renewal practices and will fine landlords $500 for the first offense and $2,500 for subsequent offenses.

extraction.ai_summary: A citizen-led committee has resubmitted a report on school bus stop safety to the College Park City Council, recommending upgrades at seven bus stops that scored low on a safety evaluation. Donald Hays, the chairman of the subcommittee, expressed frustration that the city has not acted on the recommendations, saying 'I personally feel that is an outrage and there has been very little movement and very little progress.' The committee's recommendations include adding bike lanes and sidewalks to improve safety for elementary school children waiting for their buses.

The project is a joint effort among city officials, community members, and University of Maryland partners, with a total budget of $28,382, and College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir said 'This will be a wonderful and very easy and entertaining way to know about what happened and what used to be the historic Lakeland.'

extraction.ai_summary: Three University of Maryland students, Frances Chen, Nicholas Crane, and Ashna Pandya, presented to the College Park City Council on ways to ease neighborhood tensions near the university. They suggested that the city and university could do more to help undergraduate tenants become better neighbors, citing issues like 'studentification' of established neighborhoods and high rents. As Chen said, 'We found that the most successful communities have used deep university and city cooperation to create a solution that works for both students and [homeowners] and balances affordable housing for the student population [with] enforcing city codes', while Councilmember Alan Hew emphasized that the university should take more responsibility for teaching its students how to be good neighbors.

extraction.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering expanding permit parking in residential neighborhoods. Parking Enforcement Officer Jim Miller proposed a study to determine whether to restrict parking on additional streets, stating 'It's a matter of how that restricted parking is utilized.' Councilmembers had mixed reactions to the proposal to charge for permits, with former Councilmember Susan Whitney suggesting the city offer incentives, such as tax breaks, to encourage residents to own fewer cars, saying 'We want people who need their cars to be able to park them, and we want people who can get by with one car to do that and to be encouraged to do that.'

extraction.ai_summary: The College Park City Council agreed to recommend amending a parking ordinance to allow a developer to build a four-story, 87-unit apartment building for older residents on Branchville Road. The project, called Branchville Crossing, will provide affordable housing for residents 62 and older, with rent based on 60% of the area median income, and will be financed using a 4% federal low-income housing tax credit. As Councilmember Kelly Jordan said, 'There is a real need for senior housing in College Park,' and the developer has 'listened to the residents … to see what would fit here, what’s needed here.'

extraction.ai_summary: Maryland Governor Wes Moore has proposed a bill to change Maryland zoning law to promote construction of more affordable homes by allowing developers to build at least eight houses per acre. excerpt.rendered: By Kelly Livingston  After months of work, the College Park Restorative Justice Steering Committee has presented their suggestions to the city council.  At the Nov. 2 council worksession, the committee laid out their findings as well as a framework for establishing the city’s Restorative Justice Commission, which will be tasked with addressing harms done to […]

Maxine Gross, College Park Restorative Justice Steering Committee Chair
Patrick Wojahn, College Park Mayor
organizations: College Park Restorative Justice Steering Committee
extraction.ai_summary: The College Park Restorative Justice Steering Committee has presented their suggestions to the city council, including a framework for establishing the city's Restorative Justice Commission to address harms done to the Lakeland community. As committee member Violetta Sharp-Jones explained, Lakeland was established in 1890 by African Americans seeking a better place to raise their families, but the community was later impacted by the Lakeland Urban Renewal Plan, which resulted in the destruction of ⅔ of the community and displaced over 100 families. According to Mayor Patrick Wojahn, the establishment of the commission will give people the chance to talk about what happened and 'make clear that there was real harm caused to families, caused to the community, caused to people as a result of what happened here.'

extraction.ai_summary: The College Park City Council debated a ban on gas-powered leaf blowers, sponsored by Councilmember Danny Schaible, with the goal of reducing noise and gas pollution in the city. Schaible stated, '[A gas-powered leaf blower] can be as loud as 110 decibels, 115 decibels in the ear of an operator,' and compared the pollution from using a gas-powered blower for an hour to driving a Ford F-150 for 3,000 miles. The proposed ban aims to match efforts by surrounding jurisdictions, such as University Park and Montgomery County, which have implemented or are considering similar bans.

extraction.ai_summary: The College Park City Council delayed a vote on a proposed tree ordinance after a lengthy public hearing, where around 30 residents testified, including Emily Friend, who said 'I believe if we’re going to have a successful tree planting program, it should not be punitive.' The ordinance aims to boost the city's waning tree canopy by requiring residents to obtain a free permit to cut down a tree on their property or prune one more than 20%. Todd Reitzel, a member of the Tree and Landscape Board, stated that data shows homeowners are the main contributors to tree loss, which is why the ordinance focuses on them, with most tree loss concentrated around Route 1 and Rhode Island Ave.
